food_subgroup: Pan-fried spiced mashed potato patty; 2 pieces approximately 120g per serving

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Aloo Tikki or Indian spiced potato patties. Closest parent would be a generic fried potato preparation. No defensible exact URI available; null is honest.
ontology.foodex2_description: Potato products and preparations — closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a pan-fried mashed potato patty preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Aloo Tikki. A038H (potato products and preparations) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept exists for Aloo Tikki or Indian spiced potato patty preparations.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Aloo Tikki is 2 pieces, weighing approximately 120g, as commonly served at North Indian restaurants and street food stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Aloo Tikki (spiced potato patty, pan-fried)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, which is the primary approved regional source for Indian dishes. Per-100g values are as reported. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for potato-based preparations where ICMR-NIN does not report these fields. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported by ICMR-NIN for this preparation; these fields are set to -1.0 to indicate source does not report rather than confirmed zero.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — mashed/boiled potato reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aloo Tikki as a composite preparation. GI of 74 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled and mashed potato (GI ~74–85, mean ~74 for white potato, mashed). Pan-frying the exterior does not substantially alter the glycemic response of the mashed interior. GL per 2-piece serving (120g) calculated as: (74 × 22g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.2) / 100 = approximately 19.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 200mg per 100g (240mg per 2-piece serving); chaat toppings add further sodium
hypertension.guidance: Aloo Tikki alone provides approximately 240mg sodium per 2-piece serving, which is moderate; however, when served as aloo tikki chaat with tamarind chutney and chaat masala, total sodium can exceed 600mg per serving, making it a high-sodium meal for hypertensive individuals who should limit toppings or avoid the chaat version.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~74) and high glycemic load (~19.5 per 2-piece serving) from mashed potato base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Aloo Tikki is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI (~74) and high GL (~19.5 per 2-piece serving); if consumed at a social occasion, limiting intake to 1 piece (GL ~9.8), avoiding tamarind chutney, and pairing with a protein or fibre source can partially mitigate the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.0g per 100g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat; pan-fried in modest oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aloo Tikki has a favourable cardiovascular lipid profile with zero cholesterol, zero trans fat, and only 1.0g saturated fat per 100g; the primary cardiovascular concern is the moderate sodium content and the high-carbohydrate load, which should be managed within overall daily dietary targets for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Tikki is a quintessential North Indian street food consisting of crispy pan-fried patties made from boiled and mashed potatoes seasoned with cumin, coriander, chilli, garam masala, and amchur (dried mango powder). The patties are sometimes stuffed with spiced chana dal or green peas, then shallow-fried on a tawa (iron griddle) until the exterior is golden-brown and crispy while the interior remains soft and fluffy. A standard serving is 2 pieces weighing approximately 120g. Per 100g, Aloo Tikki provides 163 kcal, 2.5g protein, 8.0g fat, and 22g carbohydrate. The primary clinical concern is the mashed potato base, which carries a high glycemic index of approximately 74 and a glycemic load of approximately 19.5 per 2-piece serving, making it a T2DM counselling priority. When served as aloo tikki chaat — topped with yoghurt, tamarind chutney, and chaat masala — the glycemic and sodium load increases substantially. Notable nutritional benefits include potassium (350mg per 100g), vitamin B6 (0.22mg per 100g), and vitamin C (8mg per 100g) from the potato base. The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free in its basic form. In Singapore, aloo tikki is available at North Indian restaurants, food courts, and hawker stalls, often served as a starter or chaat component.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN, the primary approved regional source for Indian dishes. GI value derived from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for mashed/boiled potato with documented estimation basis. All mandatory multilingual names present. Ontology anchored to closest defensible FoodEx2 parent with honest gap notes.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source — this is a plant-based preparation with no animal-derived ingredients in the standard recipe. Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based food with no animal products. Vitamin D is confirmed zero as potatoes and spices do not contribute meaningful vitamin D. Trans fat is confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source. Monounsaturated_fat_g is set to -1.0 because ICMR-NIN does not report this fatty acid fraction for this preparation; the value is not zero but simply not reported by the source. Polyunsaturated_fat_g is set to -1.0 because ICMR-NIN does not report this fatty acid fraction for this preparation; the value is not zero but simply not reported by the source. Water_g is set to -1.0 because ICMR-NIN does not report moisture content for this specific preparation; the value is not zero but simply not reported by the source. Minor micronutrients copper (0.12mg/100g), manganese (0.18mg/100g), and vitamin K (2.5mcg/100g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for potato-based preparations where ICMR-NIN does not report these fields; these are retained in source metadata but not mapped to RESULT template fields lacking direct equivalents. Portions in the input contained duplicates (three identical '1 serving 100g' entries) and multiple 'is_default: true' flags; the canonical serving of 2 pieces at 120g has been used as the primary serving size. The cultural region_tags field in the input contained a full narrative paragraph rather than structured tags; this has been normalized. Country of origin field in input was truncated; corrected to 'North India'.
